findings: Taskflow completed 12/12 paired Codex trials on the deterministic pack; baseline also completed 6/6.
The source lane found a runner-process check failure and Plugin Eval flagged manifest/privacy and progressive-disclosure issues.
The result measures the copied Codex skill overlay; native activation and workflow uplift need a task pack designed around Taskflow's planning/parallelism behavior.

V.reason: The AGENTS.md, Diataxis, and MADR composition remains valuable, but the documented Copier path renders stale content and the advertised update path fails.

U.reason: A host-neutral committed documentation and decision spine adds durable value beyond any one coding harness.

E.reason: The normal verifier passed and direct consumer, update, quoting, ShellCheck, and render probes exposed concrete failures in the shipped workflow.

X.reason: The content is host-neutral and Codex, Hermes, and Claude loading paths are evidenced, but the generator is POSIX-specific and Pi/OpenCode fit is not established.

R.reason: The documented copy command selects an eight-commit-old tag, Copier update lacks an answers file, and the verifier misses consumer-path and ignore-assertion defects.

S.reason: Users are told to grant Copier trust while shell tasks interpolate unsanitized answers; a normal apostrophe already breaks rendering and hostile values could reach shell execution.

M.reason: The repo is intentionally small, but it has no license, no working update contract, no ownership manifest, and no current release tag containing HEAD fixes.

F.reason: Codex, Hermes, and Claude have clear instruction-loading paths; OpenCode and Pi are not verified and native Windows generation is unsupported.

C.reason: A current render adds only a 2.9 KB AGENTS.md and one-line Claude include, with no skills, hooks, MCP server, telemetry, or runtime daemon.

exactCommands.command: uvx --from copier copier copy --trust --defaults --quiet --data description="Owner's library" gh:tahabakhit/agent-repo-standard /tmp/agent-repo-standard-apostrophe

opencode.reason: The repository generates no OpenCode adapter and this lane did not verify current OpenCode AGENTS.md loading behavior.

findings: The conceptual standard is lean and useful, but the current Copier implementation is not a safe default workflow.
The documented copy command selects stale v1.0.10, while the advertised update command fails because no answers file is generated.
Trusted shell tasks interpolate unsanitized answers and can clobber brownfield files outside Copier's normal merge handling.
unresolvedFacts: Native Windows generation, remote gh: rendering, brownfield adoption, OpenCode loading, and adversarial shell payloads were not executed.
runs.decision: Keep the Repo Standard concept, but stop recommending the current Copier command until tag selection, answers/update support, shell quoting, brownfield safety, verifier gaps, and licensing are fixed.
runs.removalPlan: For a generated repository, use version control to remove or restore the known scaffold files; there is no generated-file ownership manifest or automated uninstall path.

provenance.harness: Codex desktop orchestrator with independent method subagents
provenance.model: Codex desktop default model; exact model version not independently reported
provenance.sandbox: orchestrator full workspace access; source-review workers read-only against targets
provenance.network: GitHub clone and metadata only; no live model or provider benchmark

V.reason: Cross-host scaffolding can save setup work, but default ownership collisions, stale plugin metadata, and destructive updates substantially reduce realized value.

U.reason: The repo-convention cross-compiler and host-specific settings/hooks are meaningfully different from native harness features.

E.reason: Current source and 74 Python files were statically parsed, but unit dependencies, live evals, host schemas, and installs were not run.

X.reason: Nine host backends exist in source, but default outputs collide, portability is prompt-translated, and Pi and Hermes are unsupported.

R.reason: Normal init performs an unpinned dependency upgrade; force-update has no backup or three-way merge; Claude plugin metadata and install commands are stale.

S.reason: Guards deliberately fail open and are bypassable, generated permissions are broad, and the optional MCP exposes unconstrained path writes with force.

M.reason: The project is active and MIT licensed, but alpha status, one broad generator, stale metadata, and no ownership manifest or rollback path raise maintenance cost.

F.reason: Claude, Codex, and OpenCode outputs exist, but live compatibility was not verified and the Claude plugin bootstrap appears stale.

C.reason: The default creates 25 skills, multiple always-on documents, hooks, permissions, and host configurations; this is a broad repository control plane.

methods.name: source-review
methods.exactCommand: PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 python3 -c 'import ast,pathlib; files=list(pathlib.Path("src").rglob("*.py"))+list(pathlib.Path("tests").rglob("*.py")); [ast.parse(p.read_text()) for p in files]; print(f"parsed {len(files)} Python files")'

exactCommands.command: UV_PROJECT_ENVIRONMENT=/tmp/klaussy-test-venv uv run --offline --frozen --extra dev --no-editable python -m pytest -q -p no:cacheprovider tests --ignore=tests/evals --ignore=tests/e2e
PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 uv run --offline --no-project --with-editable . --with pytest --with ruff python -m pytest -q -p no:cacheprovider tests --ignore=tests/evals --ignore=tests/e2e

PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 python3 -c 'import ast,pathlib; files=list(pathlib.Path("src").rglob("*.py"))+list(pathlib.Path("tests").rglob("*.py")); [ast.parse(p.read_text()) for p in files]; print(f"parsed {len(files)} Python files")'

combinations.name: Klaussy plus Agent Repo Standard
combinations.rationale: Both want to own root instructions, repository conventions, generated skills, and update policy; Klaussy's default collision behavior makes coexistence especially risky.

findings: Default all-host generation has real AGENTS.md and .agents/skills ownership collisions.
Init always performs an unpinned dependency upgrade; force-update can overwrite broad surfaces without backup or rollback.
The guards are workflow nudges, not security boundaries, and the MCP can write caller-selected paths.
unresolvedFacts: Installability, live host compatibility, full unit tests, prompt evals, and E2E behavior were not executed.
runs.decision: Do not adopt Klaussy as the replacement repo standard or sole main plugin at v0.19.1. Re-evaluate only after ownership, reproducibility, rollback, and plugin-metadata fixes.
runs.removalPlan: No upstream uninstall or ownership manifest exists; removal requires reviewing and deleting generated files host by host, then restoring customized files from version control.

provenance.harness: Codex desktop orchestrator with independent method subagents
provenance.model: Codex desktop default model; exact model version not independently reported
provenance.sandbox: orchestrator full workspace access; source-review workers read-only against targets
provenance.network: GitHub clone and metadata only; no live model or provider benchmark

V.reason: The methodology covers planning, TDD, debugging, review, and delivery coherently, but no paired task outcomes were measured.

U.reason: Most capabilities overlap with native Codex and Claude workflows; the differentiated part is the mandatory end-to-end control policy.

E.reason: Current source, manifests, and bounded tests were inspected, but the external behavior-eval repository and live harness claims were not run.

X.reason: First-party packaging exists for Claude Code, Codex, Pi, OpenCode, and several other hosts; Hermes has no supported adapter.

R.reason: Many bounded checks passed, but Pi and Antigravity mapping tests failed and Codex packaging assertions were timezone-sensitive.

S.reason: Core hooks are local and dependency-light, but the optional visual companion executes agent-authored HTML and emits default branding telemetry; workflow skills also exercise broad git and dependency authority.

M.reason: The project is active, MIT licensed, dependency-light, and broadly tested, but cross-host removal and rollback guidance is incomplete.

F.reason: Four requested hosts have real adapters, but behavior and startup semantics differ and Hermes is unsupported.

C.reason: Plugin Eval estimated a 550-token trigger and a 31,671-token all-skills invocation ceiling; normal selective loading is lower, but the automatic methodology and subagent fan-out remain operationally heavy.

S.reason: Core hooks are local and dependency-light, but the optional visual companion executes agent-authored HTML and emits default branding telemetry; workflow skills also exercise broad git and dependency authority.

M.reason: The project is active, MIT licensed, dependency-light, and broadly tested, but cross-host removal and rollback guidance is incomplete.

F.reason: Codex has an official plugin package, but its startup behavior intentionally differs from Claude and was not run live.

C.reason: Plugin Eval estimated a 550-token trigger and a 31,671-token all-skills invocation ceiling; normal selective loading is lower, but the automatic methodology and subagent fan-out remain operationally heavy.

combinations.name: Superpowers plus Taskflow
combinations.rationale: Both control decomposition, subagents, reviews, and execution; Taskflow adds a runtime while Superpowers adds mandatory methodology.

combinations.name: Superpowers plus Klaussy
combinations.rationale: Two broad automatic control planes compete over planning, skills, hooks, review, and repository workflow.

findings: Superpowers is compact in installation size but behaviorally expansive: it is a full control plane, not a small helper.
Plugin Eval's raw 8/F is not the aggregate score; its hooks finding is likely a false positive and its deferred-token estimate includes source-only files.
Pi and Antigravity adapter tests failed at the pinned release; no live host or paired outcome run was performed.
unresolvedFacts: Actual outcome lift, token use per ordinary task, and behavior consistency across supported hosts remain unmeasured.
runs.decision: Do not use Superpowers as the sole always-on main plugin. Import selected skills or run a bounded full-methodology trial in one host.
runs.removalPlan: Use the host's plugin/package removal flow and remove any project-local .superpowers artifacts after review; upstream does not provide one complete cross-host rollback guide.

provenance.harness: Codex desktop orchestrator with independent method subagents
provenance.model: Codex desktop default model; exact model version not independently reported
provenance.sandbox: orchestrator full workspace access; source-review workers read-only against targets
provenance.network: GitHub clone and metadata only; no live model or provider benchmark

V.reason: Taskflow addresses real graph-shaped work with verification, persistence, resume, replay, and recomputation, but no paired outcome benchmark was run.

U.reason: A compiled, inspectable multi-agent DAG runtime is materially different from native one-turn subagents.

E.reason: Source, CI, host adapters, and bounded process-supervisor checks were inspected; full dependencies, live hosts, and model tasks were not run.

X.reason: First-party packages exist for Pi, Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, and Grok; Hermes is unsupported.

R.reason: The runtime has strong supervision and broad CI design, but local full tests were dependency-blocked and docs drift from implemented MCP tools.

S.reason: Filtered environments and fail-closed host runners are positives, but Pi subagents and every script phase inherit the full parent environment and run state is persisted in cleartext.

M.reason: The project is active and release automation is careful, but the runtime is large, concentrated in major files, and primarily maintained by one person.

F.reason: Four requested hosts have explicit delivery packages with meaningful feature differences; Hermes has none.

C.reason: Intermediate transcripts stay out of host context, but Plugin Eval estimated a 10,323-token Codex invocation and the runtime adds DAG authoring, persistence, MCP, and operational overhead.

combinations.name: Agent Repo Standard plus Taskflow
combinations.rationale: The repo standard owns durable structure while Taskflow is invoked only for explicit graph-shaped runtime work.

findings: Taskflow is a real orchestration runtime, not a lean routing skill.
The strongest fit is graph-shaped work; using it for ordinary single-agent tasks would add unnecessary control-plane cost.
Pi is functionally richest but has the weakest environment-isolation boundary in the reviewed source.
unresolvedFacts: No live host, provider-usage, sandbox-escape, or paired outcome benchmark was run.
runs.decision: Adopt Taskflow only for repeatable DAG-shaped work. Prefer Codex for the safest reviewed adapter; use Pi only after fixing or containing environment inheritance.
runs.removalPlan: Use each host's documented plugin/package removal path and separately decide whether to retain project-local .pi/taskflows state; Pi removal guidance is missing upstream.

provenance.harness: Codex desktop orchestrator with independent method subagents
provenance.model: Codex desktop default model; exact model version not independently reported
provenance.sandbox: orchestrator full workspace access; source-review workers read-only against targets
provenance.network: GitHub clone and metadata only; no live model or provider benchmark

V.reason: The workflow completed a real Codex contract smoke and produced a validated offline evidence ledger, but it has not yet demonstrated paired outcome lift.

U.reason: It uniquely reconciles method evidence into per-axis, per-platform, and explicit combination records while keeping evaluator-local scores separate.

E.reason: Eighteen deterministic tests and a real Promptfoo contract run exercised evidence gates, artifact verification, and orchestration semantics.

X.reason: The score model represents five harnesses, but the installed workflow and this run are Codex-native; no Claude Code, Pi, Hermes, or OpenCode adapter ran.

R.reason: The implementation passed deterministic tests, fail-closed proof checks, two independent final reviews, and one real contract smoke.

S.reason: Validated paths, symlink defenses, bounded files, digest pinning, secret-minimized subprocess environments, CSP, and read-only Promptfoo execution are implemented and tested.

M.reason: The project has no runtime dependencies and one deterministic core, but the validator-renderer script and comprehensive specification remain sizable.

F.reason: The personal Codex plugin installed and enabled successfully, its cache matched source, and the Codex app-server smoke passed.

C.reason: Plugin Eval measured a 134-token trigger and 2,014-token invocation, both moderate; its heavy deferred estimate includes repository files that are not always loaded.

methods.name: source-review
methods.exactCommand: /usr/bin/time -p sh -c 'npm test && npm run check && npm audit --omit=dev'

metrics.purpose: read-only contract smoke, not paired outcome measurement

codex.adapter: Promptfoo openai:codex-app-server:gpt-5.4 plus installed Codex plugin

V.reason: The Codex contract smoke passed, but outcome lift is not paired.

U.reason: The orchestration and ledger combination is differentiated on Codex.

E.reason: The contract and evidence gates were directly exercised.

X.reason: The data model is portable, but this adapter is Codex-specific.

R.reason: Installation, tests, and the Codex smoke passed.

S.reason: The Codex run was read-only and proof/path defenses passed tests.

M.reason: Dependency-free, with one sizable core script.

F.reason: The plugin is installed, enabled, hash-matched, and smoke-tested in Codex.

C.reason: Trigger and invocation budgets were moderate.

claude-code.reason: Version 0.1 is packaged as a Codex plugin and no Claude Code adapter ran.

pi.reason: No Pi installation or behavioral adapter exists in version 0.1.

hermes.reason: No Hermes installation or behavioral adapter exists in version 0.1.

opencode.reason: No OpenCode installation or behavioral adapter exists in version 0.1.

findings: The final Promptfoo contract smoke passed 1 of 1 trials with deterministic structural and JavaScript assertions.
Plugin Eval scored 67/D, primarily because a private local plugin intentionally lacks public privacy and terms URLs; this method score is not the aggregate score.
All 18 deterministic tests, audit, core review, and browser review passed with no remaining required findings.
Cross-platform and paired outcome value remain unmeasured.
unresolvedFacts: Outcome lift versus native Codex behavior has not been measured with paired repeated tasks.
Claude Code, Pi, Hermes, and OpenCode adapters have not been implemented or run.
Measured A+B combinations remain blocked until a four-way adapter exists.
runs.decision: Use Agent Eval now for Codex-local, evidence-labeled evaluations; do not treat this self-run as cross-harness outcome proof.
runs.removalPlan: Remove agent-eval@personal and the ~/plugins/agent-eval symlink; canonical JSON remains in this repository.
